patterns.what_to_look_for: Object-level endpoints (GET/PUT/DELETE /resource/{id}) that fetch by a client-supplied identifier without scoping the query to the authenticated principal's owner/tenant — classic BOLA/IDOR.
source_indicators: Model.findById(req.params.id) / findByPk(id) with no where:{userId:req.user.id} owner filter
Route reads req.params.id and passes straight to DAO/repository before any ownership check
Auth middleware enforces isAuthenticated() but never compares object.owner to req.user
GraphQL node(id)/Relay global-ID resolver fetching by id with no owner/tenant scope
ORM query lacks tenant_id/org_id predicate in a multi-tenant schema
blackbox_indicators: Sequential or guessable IDs in paths: /api/v1/orders/1001, /invoices/8842
Account A's token returns 200 with account B's object body
IDOR surface on /users/{id}, /documents/{id}, /payments/{id}
Response contains records whose owner != the authenticated subject
evidence_required: Two-account proof: A's token retrieving an object known to belong to B
Request/response pair showing cross-tenant/cross-user data in the body
The object ID demonstrably owned by a different principal
false_positive_checks: Confirm the object is private, not an intentionally public/shared resource
Verify the 200 actually returns B's data, not an empty/filtered body
Rule out field-level redaction or downstream gateway authz that masks the object
Ensure the ID truly maps to another tenant, not a re-used/collided value
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With account A's token, GET the object ID owned by account B and diff the returned body against B's known values.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as BOLA/IDOR with the two-account proof and exact endpoint+ID; scale severity to data sensitivity (PII/financial > metadata).

patterns.what_to_look_for: Privileged/admin action endpoints (delete user, change role, approve, export-all) that authenticate the caller but never enforce function-level role/permission — Broken Function Level Authorization.
source_indicators: Admin route missing requireRole('admin')/@PreAuthorize while sibling routes have it
Single controller serves user and admin actions; role gate only in the SPA, not server-side
Express/Fastify router mounts /admin handlers with only an auth (not authz) middleware
@PreAuthorize/@RolesAllowed annotation absent on a state-changing method
blackbox_indicators: Standard-user token calls /api/admin/*, DELETE /users/{id}, POST /roles and gets 200/204
Privileged endpoints enumerated from swagger.json or JS bundle are reachable by low-priv tokens
Action returns success for a role that has no UI affordance for it
PUT/PATCH on another principal's role/state accepted without 403
evidence_required: Low-privilege token successfully invoking a privileged action (state change or privileged 200)
Confirmation the function is privileged (admin baseline behaves differently / is gated elsewhere)
false_positive_checks: Verify the action is genuinely not permitted for that role by design
Confirm the 200 made a real change, not a no-op with authz enforced downstream
Rule out generic success on a feature-flagged/disabled endpoint
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Replay a privileged read variant (e.g., admin user-listing) with a standard-user token and check for 200 instead of 403.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as BFLA; name the privileged function, the role used, and whether it caused a state change vs privileged read; severity by blast radius.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Create/update endpoints that bind the raw request body to an ORM model with no allowlist, letting a client over-post privileged attributes (role, isAdmin, verified, balance, ownerId) — mass assignment.
source_indicators: User.update(req.body) / Object.assign(model, req.body) / new Model(req.body)
Sequelize/Mongoose create(req.body) with no fields:/pick allowlist
Spring @ModelAttribute binding the entity directly with no @JsonIgnore on sensitive fields
Rails model lacking strong params / params.permit! used
Request body spread into persistence with no DTO/whitelist layer
blackbox_indicators: Adding "role":"admin" / "isAdmin":true / "verified":true / "balance":N to a PUT/POST body persists
Undocumented field echoed back in the subsequent GET after update
Profile/settings update accepts unknown properties without a 400/422
evidence_required: Before/after showing a privileged field changed via an injected body parameter
Re-GET confirming the injected, out-of-schema field was persisted
false_positive_checks: Re-GET shows the field stripped/ignored (no persistence)
Change reverted by business logic or the field is not actually privileged
patterns.suggested_validation_task: PATCH your own record with one undocumented low-impact key, then re-GET to confirm whether unknown fields persist before touching privileged keys.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as mass assignment / over-posting; show the injected key, the persisted privileged value, and the missing allowlist; severity by which field is writable.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Responses that serialize the full backing model and leak sensitive fields (credentials, tokens, PII, internal flags) the client never needs — excessive data exposure, often relying on client-side filtering.
source_indicators: res.json(user) / return entity directly with no DTO or field projection
SELECT * then serialized to JSON
.toJSON()/serializer without hiding passwordHash/mfa_secret/tokens
GraphQL type exposing sensitive fields with no field-level auth
List endpoint returns the full object graph per item
blackbox_indicators: Response body contains password/passwordHash, mfa_secret, ssn, internal_notes, is_admin, reset_token
List endpoint returns far more per-object data than the UI renders
Other users' PII present in a collection response
Fields obviously stripped only in the SPA, not by the API
evidence_required: Response body containing a clearly sensitive field the client should not receive
false_positive_checks: Field is non-sensitive despite a scary name
Data belongs to the requesting user and disclosure is appropriate
Value is masked/tokenized/truncated rather than raw
patterns.suggested_validation_task: GET a standard resource (your user, an order list) and scan the JSON for sensitive keys beyond what the UI consumes.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as excessive data exposure; quote the leaked fields and the endpoint; severity by secret/PII class and whether other users' data is included.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Security-sensitive endpoints (login, OTP/2FA verify, password-reset, payment, invite, messaging) with no throttle, lockout, or attempt counter — unrestricted resource consumption / brute-force exposure.
source_indicators: Auth/OTP/reset route with no express-rate-limit / @RateLimiter / bucket middleware
OTP or token verification with no attempt counter or account lockout
Loop/bulk handler with no max page size or per-caller quota
No CAPTCHA/backoff on a credential-checking endpoint
blackbox_indicators: Dozens of rapid requests to /login, /verify-otp, /reset all return 200/401 with no 429
No Retry-After or X-RateLimit-* headers on sensitive routes
Short numeric OTP accepts unlimited guesses
Bulk export/pagination accepts arbitrarily large limit= without cap
evidence_required: A small controlled burst with no 429/Retry-After/lockout observed
false_positive_checks: Throttling enforced at WAF/CDN/gateway (test through the real production edge)
Account lockout or CAPTCHA triggers instead of an HTTP 429
Endpoint is not actually sensitive or is idempotent/read-only
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send a small controlled burst (~30 requests) of invalid attempts to the sensitive endpoint and check for any 429/Retry-After/lockout response.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as missing rate limit / anti-automation on a sensitive action; state observed request volume with no throttle; severity by feasibility of brute-force/abuse.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Multiple API versions / legacy or internal route prefixes where an older version lacks the authorization, tenancy, or input checks applied to the current one — version-skew auth bypass.
source_indicators: Both /api/v1 and /api/v2 mounted; v1 controller missing the authz middleware v2 has
Legacy controller retained without the new ownership/role checks
Security fix applied only to the latest handler; old route still routes to old code
Internal/beta path (/internal, /v3beta, /legacy) mounted without the standard auth chain
blackbox_indicators: /api/v1/{resource} returns data while /api/v2/{resource} enforces auth/403
Swapping the version prefix bypasses tenant or role enforcement
Deprecated version still live (200, not 404/410) and reachable
Undocumented version discovered in JS bundle/swagger responds unauthenticated
evidence_required: Same object/action accessible via the older version without the auth the new version requires
Documented diff in auth behavior between versions (403 vs 200) for equivalent requests
false_positive_checks: Confirm the old version is not actually decommissioned (404/410)
Verify both versions enforce equivalent auth (no real gap)
Rule out the legacy version being network-restricted/internal-only
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Replay an authenticated v2 request against the v1/legacy path and compare auth enforcement (403 vs 200).
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as version-skew/legacy-endpoint auth bypass; show the two versioned requests side by side and the differing enforcement; severity inherits the bypassed control.
